item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A welcome return to lolfoundation in an increasingly grimdark database. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this scip is a set of phonemes that invoke demons.

In itself, I cannot not find it too bland. That the sort of thing that I would expect as a phenomenon caused by another anomalous entity, not a whole article.

The fleshing, especially the table, would probably make a passable -J.
That it is a non-joke article, especially given the heavy LolFoundation sweat that ran down my spine while I was going through the table, definitly makes me downvote it in its current state.

I doubt this will matter much anyway given that it seems to have gotten pretty popular, but to consider removing that -1 would require that table to be gone and the joke aspect of the article to be dealt with some way or another.

This is an Euclid SCP that has been been upgraded to Keter. How ludicrous it would be for a Keter-class phenomenon to be harnessed solely for the providing of not even needed workforce, I think, doesn't need to be brought up to begin with.
This article still misses the thing that justifies its Keter upgrade. Whetever new discovery or modification of the phenomenon isn't present in the article as an addendum. If it's part of the original description of it, then it should be Keter, period.

I must admit that some of the jokes in the table were funny, which is why I would have expected it to be a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this isn't as good as many other skips by the same author, it still is enjoyable. It is though, worth noting that some of the usages listed don't make that much sense, especialy the djinn used as a lift.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The SCP Foundation: using hell for our own benefit since god knows when."
And on that note, I suddenly have the urge to write a tale around this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Proposals to address astronomical conditions are under consideration.

Hell of a way to end an article. I love it when the Foundation's hubris is put on display in absolute deadpan fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, downvote for me. As good as the reference is, it is a poor execution to me.

Go one step beyond, be more blatant on the humorous side, and maybe this can be a good -J.

But for a mainlist article, it needs to be greatly changed. It could be completely contained, and then have a good and long exploration log about the Foundation hunting users of this ritual across the globe. Then we could have all those demonic-powered devices showing up with even more punch to each, and the slow discovery of the circumstances that increase the power of the gate.

It could be so much more, but as is this is extremely unprofessional and out of place for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation has developed a teratogen and introduced it to the water supply in the geographic area where most of the population of haplogroup ███-██ resides, for the purpose of causing individuals born in that area to have congenital anatomical defects which render them physically unable to vocalize the phonemes.

Jesus fucking christ

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: AJMansfield mentioned up thread a similarity to the idea of demonics in SCP-2167 and A Brief Explanation on Demonics, as well as later articles such as Operation LLEWYN DARK and Hypervelocity. There could be a crosslink here indicating that PL has/had comparable but smaller-scale technology.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I've already added a crosslink, above the test log.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I see, in that case what about a 4th list item about using your 001 as a safe testing ground?

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Rejected. The use of "Location T" in SCP-2336 and "Facility T" in spikebrennan's proposal is actually an inadvertent coincidence - it doesn't make any sense for them to be the same thing (since spikebrennan's proposal certainly doesn't talk about putting a landfill at Facility T). I'll fix this article to avoid confusion.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide

To link to the mentioned Facility T that features prominently in the author's 001 proposal, the following link is proposed:

Access to Location T has been restricted by placing a landfill at the site.

to

Access to Location T has been restricted by placing a landfill at the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late to the chain, but yeah. It is a recent thing. Cause murica is kool, so we make new stuff from famed old stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

It's a great example of the Foundation exploiting SCPs, and the cross-references (explicit and non-explicit) really add to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Howard-Stross gateway

oh u

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that a recent thing? Mostly contemporary American tribulation eschatology, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to add, 216 is 6 * 6 * 6 and is thus sometimes symbolic of the Mark of the Beast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The number of the beast is 216, not 666. Unless you were intentionally going for the "Prince of Lies" angle, 666 doesn't work theologically.

Well, 616 is also known as a contender for the Number of the Beast and Wikipedia pits "666" and "616" as the strongest possibilities. That said, "666" remains the most common and salient in pop culture when one thinks of the Devil's number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. Not bad, not outstanding. I was amused by some of the jokes, especially, the Maxwell/Laplace bits, and I didn't mind the idea. But it didn't really feel like either a great realistic piece or a great comic piece. I didn't like A666 for a couple of reasons:

1. The number of the beast is 216, not 666. Unless you were intentionally going for the "Prince of Lies" angle, 666 doesn't work theologically.

2. There are so many SCPs that reference religion in various ways. I frankly think that it's bad form to give Satan (or any other prominent biblical figure) an SCP number, even if it's kind of a joke. So many people have this idea, and it's central enough to (Western) culture that it starts to turn into a "gimmick" pretty fast, which just causes problems for the cases where someone would do it seriously and well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You really don't want to see another lolFoundation era.
Funny articles are okay, but lolFoundation is just no.
Try the History Of The Universe series by Roget for more information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the whole [demons × magic × tech] thing. +1 from me here.

This also fits perfectly alongside two articles I wrote, SCP-2167 and A Brief Explanation of Demonics, which were inspired by the idea of using an actual demon (Maxwell's or otherwise) as part of an electronic device, to the point of using the "electrodemonic effect" and having "demon gate junction transistors" that incorporate micron-scale demons, rather than the meter-scale demons you use in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is way too LOLFoundation, if it were more audacious it'd be a great Joke article.

I'm sure The Ethics Committee has a lot to say about enslaving sapient anomalies for the most mundane purposes. Enslaving persons for good reasons is stilled fucked up from a game theory perspective, what's next, enslaving angels and making pillows from their wings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not digging this at all. The fact that it's a demon summoning ritual that the Foundation is using for practical purposes goes against the purpose of the Foundation entirely. They're not trying to advance society or turn a profit based on anomalous objects, they're trying to keep them out of the hands of normal people. In the case of Thaumiel-class items (ones that are useful for containing other objects), I think it's better to limit those to items either the Foundation themselves have created or are well-documented objects created by their contemporaries. The fact that they found out there are Keter-level threats associated with the ritual shows them being uncharacteristically quick to adopt the usage of a poorly-understood item for convenience.

I'm not sure that it even needs the Keter class. It might even be safe, since containment boils down to "don't speak the eldritch words", even if there are uncontained instances of the ritual. This is an instance where they just need to track down anyone who might have a copy of a book or scroll or whatever that can communicate the words needed and put it in a box. Then bury the box, in the case of people.

The multiple uses of creatures summoned makes the whole thing seem like LOLFoundation tales that we've long since moved away from. I think it better that their abilities were discovered in experiments then immediately destroyed. The last one in particular irks me a LOT, since it makes the whole thing seem like a convenient way to save people doomed in another article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what Dwarf Fortress has to do with it but:

And the jinn did He create of smokeless fire.
- Qu'ran 55:15

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't hold my attention. It's an idea that is pretty bland in and of itself (demons summoned for mundane purposes) and the presentation is so terse and clipped that the article doesn't produce anything all that interesting. The closest this comes to interesting me is the references to Maxwell and Laplace's demon, but that's embellishment on content which doesn't intrigue me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i was hoping this was some sort of dwarf fortress thingy with one being made of smoke and stuff. ah well.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the underlying humour of this. LolFoundation (whatever that is, in fact!)? I don't know. Funny? Hell yeah!

You had me at this:

The formation of such a gateway frequently results in the arrival of a Type I or Type II tartarean entity

A jewel of clinical tone!

And I like the originality of it. This 'thing' is Keter, so it's logical to assume that the beings it summons are Keter too… but no, the Foundation is whistling them into service. Getting cocky and overconfident (which is also a subtext in 1844). "No worry, everything's under control…" "Yeah. You hope."

Note to self: remember to visit Spikebrennan's member page. I want more of the same!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really surprised this is getting upvotes.

I very strongly dislike this article. Since when does the Foundation kidnap entities and put them to work, let alone sapient creatures? Demons powering turbines? Seriously? The chances of a containment breach increase astronomically when you have anomalous entities responsible for the functioning of the Site in which they are contained. We're going to make something a slave and have them be responsible for moving personnel in an elevator or powering a site in which we use that power to contain other scips and not expect that to blow up in our faces? How is this much different than the "free to roam the facility" days? Even if we are somehow harnessing their power without their knowledge, it is taking an incredible risk by integrating anomalous entities into structure systems. These things are magic, for crying out loud. They could do any number of unpredictable things and compromise containment of everything at the site.

I feel like this borders on lolFoundation, something i really loathe. The Foundation in my headcannon wouldn't use demons to make fertilizer any more than they would use a scip for zany pranks. This feels like a silly intro in some kind of Men In Black-like film in which our main characters walk around the facility and we see in the background that, oh my!, the elevator is powered by a demon. Bleh.

The Foundation doesn't use. It contains. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the applications log myself… the idea that the entities are being put to use in carefully controlled and monitored situations actually sits well with my personal headcanon: That is to say, the Foundation has whole sites full of well-studied anomalous entities that can be managed under standard containment procedures. It's the ones that do something interesting, or that are annoyingly hard to contain like SCP-106, or that are poorly understood that get special containment procedures.

I like to think of the Foundation's approach to making use of an anomaly as somewhat similar to running a nuclear power plant- you can never leave one unsupervised, take it's security and safety precautions lightly, or forget that every aspect of it, from fuel to high pressure steam pipes to waste, is capable of killing you in some way… but they're still a well understood, reliable, and efficient power source.

The elevator thing, for instance, wouldn't make sense for a typical elevator, but if we're talking about, say, a freight elevator for a major underground facility a kilometer or so underground used to contain far more dangerous entities, it might only be possible to efficiently build such a thing by tightly controlled use of a well understood anomaly with multiple redundant safety systems to keep it under control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just can't get behind this one, primarily due to the table.

At the base of it, demon-summoning-played-straight is kind of a bland starting point. You've done very well fleshing it out and realizing it despite that. I especially love the extra containment procedures, even if they're about summoning the generic devil.

I'm just not down with the Foundation regularly summoning and entrapping extradimensional beings through an anomalous process for mundane tasks. Experimentation to understand the bounds of the anomaly? Sure. Potentially destabilising reality to power an elevator? No. (Physics jokes or not.)

Given that you've crosslinked to a permanent hell-portal, you'd think they would know better.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This fixed all the problems I had with the last version. The nods to Maxwell's and Laplace's demons were nice touches too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This reads like the prequel to the award-winning thriller flick "The Foundation Fights Ten Billion Demons" featuring John Doomguy and the burning remains of Site-32.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some part of me was disappointed that a phoneme-based skip that opened portals was just your average demon summoning.

But the things we're doing with the demons, my god! I kind of feel sorry for them! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this version much more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I like this. In particular the invisible being of smokeless flame.

It was a real tonic.

Upvoted for being pretty damn cool in my eyes. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I swear, the longer I have my demon-summoning GPS devices languishing in my sandbox, the more outmoded they become.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish there was more but I really like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It still feels a bit too flimsy of sorts to me, as if there could still be expansion here and there. If you can find an area where you feel you can work on a bit, I'd recommend doing so. However, the crosslink to 1844 really works, and helps contextualize the piece as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted as the table is a definite improvement. The mundane trivialization of goetic mysteries is great epistemological horror. I do find the Robert Howard Charles Stross gates to be a little too obvious of a literary reference though, but not enough to affect my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh my god the foundation is using beelzebub to make fertilizer
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

But I think you should change "Application" to "Experimental Application."

At the very least, change the elevator one. Using a Demon to power an elevator just to save a few dollars off the electric bill seems like a gross breach of Foundation etiquette and security.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I already liked this before, with my only issue being with the execution. While I still think that more could be done here, this version does feel more complete.

Also, it would be a crime to downvote a joke about Maxwell's Demon.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted due almost entirely to Maxwell's and Laplace's demons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SO much more with the included table. I liked it before, but this is awesome!

+1

EDIT: Sorry guys, this is slightly random, but if someone could tell me what my current Karma level is, I would appreciate it. It's been fluctuating between low and high all day and I want to make sure it's just Wikidot being a bitch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Used as gas filter in modified Maxwell engine to overcome thermodynamics.

Let me guess… Maxwell's demon!

Consciousness imprinted into Laplace processor for computational purposes.

And this… Laplace's demon!

Sapient. Invisible (body plan assumed to be bipedal and winged). Capable of communicating in archaic Arabic. During interviews, claims to be composed of "smokeless flame of fire." Capable of levitating.

A djinn?
